hook: You already know how to find the area of a rectangle — but what about a circle, a cone, or a sphere? Flat shapes are just the beginning; the real world is three-dimensional, and most objects you encounter have curved surfaces and hidden volumes. In this unit, you'll build the tools to measure virtually any shape nature or engineering can throw at you.
concept: Every topic in this unit asks the same fundamental question: how much space does a shape occupy, whether spread across a flat surface, wrapped around an exterior, or filled in three dimensions? Together these ideas fall under the umbrella of geometric measurement — capturing size through area, surface area, and volume.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Area of Triangles, Quadrilaterals, and Polygons
topic_roadmap.description: You will master the area formulas for triangles, parallelograms, trapezoids, and general polygons, building the 2-D foundation everything else in this unit rests on.
You will calculate a circle's circumference $C = 2\pi r$ and area $A = \pi r^2$, unlocking the measurement of every curved flat region.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Surface Area of Prisms and Cylinders
topic_roadmap.description: You will find the total surface area of prisms and cylinders by unfolding their faces into nets and summing the areas of rectangles and circles.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Surface Area of Pyramids and Cones
topic_roadmap.description: You will compute the lateral and total surface areas of pyramids and cones using slant height, connecting flat polygon formulas to pointed 3-D solids.
You will apply the principle that volume equals base area times height, $V = Bh$, to find how much space prisms and cylinders enclose.
You will discover why pyramids and cones hold exactly one-third the volume of their prism and cylinder counterparts, using $V = \tfrac{1}{3}Bh$.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Surface Area and Volume of Spheres
topic_roadmap.description: You will use the formulas $SA = 4\pi r^2$ and $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ to measure the curved surface and interior volume of spheres.

You will apply Cavalieri's Principle to explain why two solids with equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas at every level must have equal volumes, giving a rigorous foundation to all the volume formulas you've used.
